title: Virtual-thread use cases

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Virtual-thread use cases; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; JVM/runtime concepts, Concurrency, and Virtual Threads learning paths; atomic topic: Virtual-thread use cases
sources.claim: Java virtual threads make large numbers of mostly blocking tasks affordable while preserving the thread-per-task style; they do not create more CPU execution capacity.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java virtual threads make large numbers of mostly blocking tasks affordable while preserving the thread-per-task style; they do not create more CPU execution capacity.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Each independent network request runs in a virtual thread while a semaphore limits the database connections they may occupy. Your goal is to choose between sequential, platform-thread, virtual-thread and stream-based execution for a stated workload. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Packages, builds and tests; List, Set, Map and Queue; Threads, tasks and executors; Race conditions and synchronization. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java virtual threads make large numbers of mostly blocking tasks affordable while preserving the thread-per-task style; they do not create more CPU execution capacity. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: CPU-bound work still needs bounded parallelism, scarce external resources need limits, pinning or thread-local usage can affect scale, cancellation remains cooperative, and task lifetime needs structure. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Virtual threads make CPU-bound code run in parallel without limits and remove the need for timeouts, cancellation, or resource bounds.

includes: Use virtual threads for numerous blocking tasks while distinguishing them from CPU parallelism.
Choose between sequential, platform-thread, virtual-thread and stream-based execution for a stated workload.
excludes: CPU-bound work still needs bounded parallelism, scarce external resources need limits, pinning or thread-local usage can affect scale, cancellation remains cooperative, and task lifetime needs structure.

outcomes: Choose between sequential, platform-thread, virtual-thread and stream-based execution for a stated workload.

examples.title: Virtual-thread use cases: direct language case
examples.situation: Each independent network request runs in a virtual thread while a semaphore limits the database connections they may occupy.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: Each independent network request runs in a virtual thread while a semaphore limits the database connections they may occupy.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use virtual threads for numerous blocking tasks, but still bound scarce resources and use CPU-appropriate parallelism for compute-bound work.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use virtual threads for numerous blocking tasks, but still bound scarce resources and use CPU-appropriate parallelism for compute-bound work.

examples.title: Virtual-thread use cases: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A CPU-heavy image transform uses a bounded executor sized for processors instead of launching thousands of virtual threads.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A CPU-heavy image transform uses a bounded executor sized for processors instead of launching thousands of virtual threads.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: CPU-bound work still needs bounded parallelism, scarce external resources need limits, pinning or thread-local usage can affect scale, cancellation remains cooperative, and task lifetime needs structure.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Virtual threads make CPU-bound code run in parallel without limits and remove the need for timeouts, cancellation, or resource bounds.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose between sequential, platform-thread, virtual-thread and stream-based execution for a stated workload.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Virtual threads make CPU-bound code run in parallel without limits and remove the need for timeouts, cancellation, or resource bounds.

analogy.example: a dispatch center that tracks reachable work orders and assigns bounded tasks while protecting shared ledgers can introduce Virtual-thread use cases, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: reachability, executors, races, and virtual-thread workload choice
limitations: memory visibility, scheduling, atomicity, cancellation, and garbage-collection timing cannot be inferred from human dispatch
